items.title: Text-based PDF extraction via the content stream
items.description: Born-digital PDFs carry a logical text layer in their content streams, so extraction should read that layer directly rather than rasterizing and re-recognizing it. Use a layout-aware parser that preserves reading order, word and line boundaries, and byte offsets back to the originating object. Direct text extraction is faster, lossless for the encoded glyphs, and avoids the error surface of OCR entirely.
items.applicability: Ingesting machine-generated banking documents such as exported statements, system reports, and contracts produced by reporting engines.
items.example: A 40-page EF Core migration report exported to PDF is parsed with a content-stream reader; each extracted paragraph retains its source page index and a character span used later for citation anchors.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: keep page-level references and the original document hash for every extracted span, and record that this text is extracted (not model-generated). Embedded fonts with broken ToUnicode maps can yield garbled glyphs, so flag low-confidence decode runs for human review on legal or financial documents.

items.title: Scanned PDF OCR with per-token confidence
items.description: Scanned or image-only PDFs have no text layer, so each page must be rasterized at sufficient resolution and recognized with an OCR engine that emits bounding boxes and per-token confidence. Pre-processing — deskew, denoise, binarize — materially improves accuracy on faxed or photocopied banking records. The OCR result is a reconstruction, not ground truth, and must be tagged as such throughout the pipeline.
items.applicability: Onboarding legacy scanned cheque images, signed paper contracts, and archived correspondence into the knowledge base.
items.example: A scanned ChequeXpert batch is rendered at 300 DPI, deskewed, then OCR'd; tokens below 0.70 confidence on the payee line are highlighted for manual verification before any downstream use.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: persist page-level bounding boxes, the OCR confidence per token, and the document hash. Distinguish OCR-extracted text from any model summary, and require human review for legal or financial documents because OCR misreads (0/O, 1/l, decimal points) carry real monetary risk.

items.title: Mixed PDF handling — per-page extraction routing
items.description: Real-world PDFs frequently mix born-digital pages with scanned inserts, so routing must be decided per page rather than per document. Detect a usable text layer on each page; route pages with extractable text to the content-stream reader and text-less pages to OCR. Recording which path produced each span keeps the provenance chain honest about extraction quality.
items.applicability: Processing composite loan files where a typed agreement is followed by scanned signature pages and stamped annexes.
items.example: A 12-page MCIB submission has pages 1 through 9 extracted from the text layer and pages 10 through 12 routed to OCR; each resulting chunk is tagged with its extraction method.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: tag every span with its page number, extraction method (text vs OCR), and confidence, and keep the original document hash. Treat OCR pages as lower trust and require human review for legal or financial documents.

items.title: Table extraction with cell-level coordinates
items.description: Tabular data in PDFs lacks an explicit grid model, so tables must be reconstructed from ruling lines or whitespace alignment into rows, columns, and cells. Preserve the geometric coordinates of each cell so a value can be traced back to its exact position on the page. Numeric fidelity matters more than layout aesthetics for financial tables, so the extractor should validate column totals where present.
items.applicability: Pulling structured figures out of trial balances, amortization schedules, and reconciliation reports.
items.example: A BDM settlement statement table is extracted to rows; each cell retains its page and bounding box, and a detected column total is cross-checked against the sum of its rows.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: store cell-level page references and coordinates plus the document hash, and keep extracted cell values separate from any model interpretation. Merged cells and multi-line headers cause misalignment, so financial tables require human review before figures are trusted.

items.title: Embedded image extraction and classification
items.description: PDFs embed images — diagrams, charts, signatures, stamps, logos — that carry information the text layer does not. Extract embedded raster and vector objects with their page placement, then classify them so downstream consumers can decide whether to OCR a chart caption or treat a signature as a non-text artifact. Images are evidence and must inherit the same provenance discipline as text.
items.applicability: Capturing architecture diagrams from technical PDFs and detecting signature or stamp regions in executed contracts.
items.example: An ETAMS design PDF yields three embedded diagrams; each is stored with its page index and a classifier label of diagram, while a signature block on the last page is flagged as a non-text region.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: keep each image's page reference, position, and the parent document hash. Do not let a model invent the contents of an image it cannot read; require human review where image content (signatures, stamps) is legally significant.

items.title: Document metadata extraction and trust scoring
items.description: PDF metadata — author, producer, creation and modification dates, XMP fields, and digital signatures — provides context that text extraction alone does not. Capture both the document information dictionary and XMP stream, and verify any embedded signatures to establish authenticity. Metadata can be absent or forged, so it informs trust scoring rather than guaranteeing it.
items.applicability: Establishing the origin and integrity of imported banking documents for the audit trail.
items.example: An imported contract reports a Producer of a known reporting engine and a valid embedded signature, raising its trust score; another with a mismatched modification date is flagged for review.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: record extracted metadata verbatim alongside the document hash and never let the model fabricate missing fields. Metadata is advisory; require human review for legal or financial documents where authenticity is in question.

items.title: Document classification before summarization
items.description: Documents should be classified by type — contract, statement, technical spec, correspondence — before any summarization, because the downstream prompt, chunking, and review policy depend on the class. Classification combines structural signals (metadata, layout, table density) with content signals to produce a label and a confidence. Routing by class ensures legal and financial documents receive stricter handling than informational ones.
items.applicability: Triaging a mixed import batch so contracts go to the legal-review queue and technical PDFs go to the engineering knowledge base.
items.example: An imported PDF is classified as loan-agreement with 0.88 confidence, which automatically enables mandatory human review and page-level citation requirements for its summary.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: persist the classification label, its confidence, and the features that drove it alongside the document hash. A low-confidence or financial classification must escalate to human review, and the model summary remains distinct from the extracted text.

items.title: Provenance-preserving chunking strategy
items.description: Chunking for retrieval and summarization should respect document structure — sections, paragraphs, table boundaries — rather than splitting on a fixed character count that severs sentences. Every chunk must carry its page range, character span, and extraction method so a downstream citation can resolve to an exact location. Structure-aware chunks improve retrieval relevance and make summaries verifiable against the source.
items.applicability: Preparing extracted PDF content for embedding, retrieval, and grounded summarization.
items.example: A contract is chunked by clause; clause 7.3 becomes one chunk tagged with page 5, its character offsets, and the text extraction method, ready for citation in a summary.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: each chunk must retain page-level references, character spans, and the document hash. Chunk boundaries must not blur which page a fact came from, and the extracted chunk text stays separate from any model-generated summary.

items.title: Grounded summarization with mandatory citations
items.description: Summaries must be generated only from retrieved, extracted chunks and must cite the page and span supporting each claim, so the model is constrained to grounded content rather than free recall. The summarization prompt should instruct the model to abstain when the source does not support a statement. A summary without citations is not acceptable output for banking documents.
items.applicability: Producing executive summaries of contracts and technical specifications for reviewers.
items.example: A summary sentence reads 'The facility matures on 30 June 2027 [p.3]', where the bracketed citation resolves to the exact extracted span on page 3.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: every claim carries a page-level reference and the document hash is retained. The model summary is stored separately from the extracted text, hallucination risk is non-zero so uncited claims are rejected, and legal or financial summaries require human review before use.

items.title: Citation preservation through the pipeline
items.description: A citation must survive every transformation from raw extraction through chunking, embedding, retrieval, and summary rendering without losing its link to the source page and span. Treat the citation as an immutable identifier attached to each unit of content and propagated, never recomputed by guesswork. This end-to-end fidelity is what lets a reviewer click a summary claim and land on the exact source location.
items.applicability: Enabling click-through verification from a generated summary back to the originating PDF page.
items.example: Chunk c-0042 carries citation {hash, page 5, span 1180-1240}; after retrieval and summarization the rendered claim still references c-0042, resolving to that page and offset.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: the page-level reference and document hash must be carried, not regenerated, at each stage. Broken or inferred citations are treated as failures, and any claim that loses its citation is excluded from legal or financial outputs.

items.title: Hallucination risk controls for document summaries
items.description: Language models can assert plausible but unsupported facts, so summarization of banking documents must constrain generation to retrieved evidence and verify each claim against its cited span. Apply grounding checks — claim-to-source entailment, citation existence, and numeric consistency — and reject or flag claims that fail. The goal is to make unsupported assertions detectable and to keep the human reviewer in the loop where stakes are high.
items.applicability: Guarding contract and financial-statement summaries against fabricated terms, figures, or dates.
items.example: A draft summary asserts an interest rate not present in any extracted chunk; the grounding check finds no supporting span and removes the claim before the summary is shown.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: every retained claim has a page-level citation and the document hash is preserved. Hallucination risk cannot be reduced to zero, so the model summary stays distinct from extracted text and legal or financial documents always require human review.

items.title: Extraction confidence scoring and thresholds
items.description: Every extracted unit should carry a confidence score derived from its source method — high for clean text-layer spans, variable for OCR tokens, lower for reconstructed tables. Confidence drives routing decisions: high-confidence content can summarize automatically while low-confidence content is held for review. Surfacing confidence to reviewers prevents silent reliance on shaky extractions.
items.applicability: Deciding which extracted content is trustworthy enough for automated downstream use.
items.example: OCR tokens on a faded statement average 0.62 confidence, which falls below the 0.80 threshold and routes the page to manual transcription before its figures enter a summary.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: store the confidence with the page-level reference and document hash. Confidence is a signal, not a guarantee, so financial figures below threshold require human review and extracted content remains separate from any model output.

items.title: Page-level provenance as the minimum citation unit
items.description: Page number is the floor of acceptable provenance for any extracted fact, with character spans or bounding boxes preferred where the extraction method supports them. Page-level references let a reviewer locate the source quickly and are achievable even on OCR'd pages where finer offsets are unreliable. No fact should enter a summary without at least a page anchor and a link to the document hash.
items.applicability: Guaranteeing that every summarized statement is traceable to a specific page of a specific document.
items.example: An OCR'd contract cannot yield reliable character offsets, so its summary claims cite page numbers; a born-digital section cites page plus span for tighter traceability.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: page-level references are mandatory and the original document hash is always kept. Where finer spans are unavailable the page anchor still applies, and legal or financial documents require human review of the cited pages.

items.title: Redaction detection and irreversible masking
items.description: Redaction in PDFs is frequently fake — a black rectangle drawn over still-extractable text — so the pipeline must detect overlay-only redactions and avoid surfacing the underlying content. When the platform itself redacts, masking must remove the text from the content stream rather than merely covering it visually. Detecting and honoring redactions protects sensitive banking data from leaking through extraction.
items.applicability: Handling documents with redacted account numbers or personal data during import and summarization.
items.example: A contract shows black boxes over account numbers; the extractor detects extractable text beneath the overlays and refuses to surface it, logging a redaction-integrity warning.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: record where redactions occurred at page level and keep the document hash, but never include masked content in a summary. Improper redaction is a data-leak risk, so flagged documents require human review before any legal or financial use.

items.title: Privacy and PII handling in document intelligence
items.description: Extracted PDF content commonly contains personal and financial identifiers, so the pipeline must detect PII, minimize its exposure, and gate it behind access controls. Sensitive fields should be tokenized or masked in derived artifacts such as embeddings and summaries while the original remains under restricted access. Privacy handling is local-first and must function without any external service.
items.applicability: Processing customer statements and KYC documents that carry names, account numbers, and identifiers.
items.example: A statement's account number is detected as PII and replaced with a stable token in the summary and embedding, while the original value stays only in the access-controlled extracted store.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: keep page-level references and the document hash even for masked fields. PII detection is imperfect, so the model summary excludes raw identifiers and legal or financial documents require human review before release.

items.title: Audit trail for extraction and summarization
items.description: Every document that enters the pipeline must produce an append-only audit record capturing its hash, extraction methods per page, classification, model and prompt versions used for summarization, and reviewer actions. The audit trail makes the provenance chain durable and answers later questions about how a given summary was produced. It is the system of record for compliance review.
items.applicability: Demonstrating to auditors how a contract summary was generated and who approved it.
items.example: The audit record for a loan file shows pages 1 to 9 text-extracted, 10 to 12 OCR'd, classified as loan-agreement, summarized by a named model version, and approved by a named reviewer with a timestamp.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: the audit record binds page-level references and the document hash to every step and is append-only. It separates extracted text from model summary, and confirms that legal or financial documents passed human review before release.

items.title: Multi-document comparison with cross-references
items.description: Comparing related PDFs — versions of a contract, a statement against its reconciliation — requires aligning sections across documents and reporting differences with citations into each source. Alignment should match on structure and content rather than position, since layouts diverge between versions. Each reported difference must reference the page and span in both documents so the comparison is verifiable.
items.applicability: Diffing two contract revisions or reconciling a vendor statement against an internal ledger export.
items.example: Comparing contract v1 and v2 surfaces a changed termination clause, citing page 6 span in v1 and page 7 span in v2, with both document hashes recorded.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: every reported difference carries page-level references into both documents and both document hashes. The comparison output is a model artifact kept distinct from extracted text, and legal or financial differences require human review.

items.title: Report generation from extracted PDF content
items.description: Generating a structured report from one or more PDFs assembles extracted facts and grounded summaries into a templated output where every populated field links back to its source. Computed values such as totals are derived from extracted figures, not invented, and carry their input citations. The report is a presentation layer over the provenance chain, never a replacement for it.
items.applicability: Producing a one-page due-diligence brief from a folder of contracts and financial statements.
items.example: A due-diligence report's maturity-date field shows '30 June 2027' with a citation to page 3 of the source contract, and its facility-amount field cites the extracted figure on page 2.
items.limitation: Never summarize without provenance: every report field carries a page-level citation and the source document hash, and computed figures cite their inputs. Generated narrative is distinct from extracted text, hallucination risk applies to free-text sections, and legal or financial reports require human review before distribution.
